# CoherentStep
<!-- Suggested placement: Chapter 5, Algorithm subsection, immediately after Algorithm 1 discussion (p. 86) -->
<!-- Depends on: KMS Lindbladian (§5.2, eqs 5.28-5.30), Time-domain representations (eqs 5.38-5.43), Quadrature errors for B (eqs 5.61-5.78), Hamiltonian Trotterization (Prop 10, eq 5.81), Generator splitting (eqs 5.90-5.93) -->

**Coherent step: block encoding and Hamiltonian simulation via GQSP.**  The CoherentStep (Algorithm 1a) implements the unitary evolution $e^{-i\delta B_a}$ generated by the per-jump coherent term $B_a$.  This is the component that distinguishes the CKG algorithm from the CKBG construction: without it (i.e.\ setting $B_a = 0$ for all $a$), the algorithm reduces to the CKBG Gibbs sampler \cite{chen2023quantum}, which satisfies only approximate GNS detailed balance.  Recall from the KMS Lindbladian subsection that the coherent term $G$ was derived (equation (5.28)) precisely to cancel the anti-Hermitian residual in the quantum discriminant, upgrading the Lindbladian from approximate GNS to exact KMS detailed balance.  Its per-jump contribution $B_a$ enters the coherent--dissipative splitting (5.90) as the generator $\mathcal{L}_{a,\mathrm{coh}}(\cdot) = -i[B_a, \cdot]$, with the first-order splitting error of $\mathcal{O}(\delta^2)$ already matched by the weak-measurement error of the dissipative step.  Of course, discretisation and Trotterisation errors analysed in the preceding subsections will re-introduce controlled violations of this detailed balance --- but these are precisely the errors we have learned to bound.

The algorithm proceeds in two stages: first we construct a *block encoding* $U_{B_a}$ satisfying
$$
\langle 0|_{T_-}\langle 0|_{T_+}\,U_{B_a}\,|0\rangle_{T_-}|0\rangle_{T_+} = B_a/\alpha,
$$
where $\alpha = \|b_-\|_1\,\|b_+\|_1$ is the subnormalisation factor; then we use GQSP \cite{motlagh2024generalized} to implement $e^{-i\delta B_a}$ from $U_{B_a}$.  We describe each stage in turn.

\medskip

**Block encoding via nested LCU.**  The block encoding is the heart of the CoherentStep and represents, to the best of our knowledge, the first fully explicit circuit-level description of this construction.  The CKG paper \cite{chen2023efficient} establishes the existence of such a block encoding (Proposition III.1 and Figure 4 therein) and proves the correct asymptotic scaling, but the full nested double-integral structure with two time registers is left implicit.  Here we unpack it completely.

The starting point is the nested double-integral representation of $B_a$ from the Time, time, time subsection (equation (5.39)):
$$
B = \sum_{a\in\mathcal{A}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} b_-(t)\,e^{-iHt/\sigma}\left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} b_+(t')\,A^{a\dagger}(\beta t')\,A^a(-\beta t')\,\mathrm{d}t'\right]e^{iHt/\sigma}\,\mathrm{d}t.
$$
That this expression factorises into an outer integral (over $t$) and an inner integral (over $t'$) is a direct consequence of the Kossakowski matrix splitting (5.38):
$$
\frac{1}{2\pi}\,\alpha_{\nu_1,\nu_2} = \hat f_+(\nu_+)\,\hat f_-(\nu_-), \qquad \nu_\pm := \nu_1 \pm \nu_2,
$$
which decouples the two-dimensional Fourier transform over the Bohr frequencies $(\nu_1, \nu_2)$ into two independent one-dimensional transforms.  The outer kernel $b_-$ comes from $\hat f_-(\nu_-)$ and encodes the off-diagonal suppression and the coherent-term structure (the $\tanh$ piece from (5.28)); it is universal across all transition weight choices $\gamma$.  The inner kernel $b_+$ comes from $\hat f_+(\nu_+)$ and carries all dependence on the transition weight.  This clean separation is what makes the circuit construction possible.

After discretisation (Quadrature errors for $B$ subsection, equation (5.61)), the continuous operator $B$ is approximated by
$$
\bar{B} = \sum_a\, t_0 \sum_{\bar t}\, b_-(\bar t)\,e^{-iH\bar t/\sigma}\left[t_0' \sum_{\bar t'}\, b_+(\bar t')\,A^{a\dagger}(\beta\bar t')\,A^a(-\beta\bar t')\right]e^{iH\bar t/\sigma},
$$
with the Riemann-sum prefactors $t_0, t_0'$ kept explicit (unlike the dissipative part, where they are absorbed into the filter function $\bar f$).  The Linear Combination of Unitaries (LCU) framework --- introduced for Hamiltonian simulation in \cite{childs2012hamiltonian,berry2015simulating} and formalised as a block-encoding primitive in \cite{gilyen2019quantum} --- turns this double sum into a state-preparation / controlled-evolution / un-preparation sandwich on two ancilla registers: an outer time register $T_-$ of $r_-$ qubits and an inner time register $T_+$ of $r_+$ qubits.

Steps 2 and 4 of Algorithm 1a prepare these registers:
$$
|0\rangle_{T_-} \;\longrightarrow\; |b_-\rangle := \sum_{\bar t}\, \sqrt{\frac{t_0\,|b_-(\bar t)|}{\alpha_-}}\;\mathrm{sgn}\!\big(b_-(\bar t)\big)\,|\bar t\rangle, \qquad \alpha_- = \|b_-\|_1,
$$
$$
|0\rangle_{T_+} \;\longrightarrow\; |b_+\rangle := \sum_{\bar t'}\, \sqrt{\frac{t_0'\,|b_+(\bar t')|}{\alpha_+}}\;\mathrm{sgn}\!\big(b_+(\bar t')\big)\,|\bar t'\rangle, \qquad \alpha_+ = \|b_+\|_1.
$$
The absolute values go into the amplitudes (ensuring a valid quantum state with $\langle b_\pm | b_\pm \rangle = 1$) and the signs go into the phases.  For the outer kernel $b_-$ (equation (5.40)), which is real-valued, $\mathrm{sgn}(b_-(\bar t)) \in \{-1,+1\}$ is the ordinary sign function.  For the inner kernel $b_+^{(s,\eta)}$ (equation (5.43)), which is complex-valued in general, the notation $\mathrm{sgn}(b_+(\bar t'))$ should be understood as the unit-modulus phase $b_+(\bar t')/|b_+(\bar t')|$.  The same convention applies to the Gaussian case (5.41), where $b_+$ involves the complex exponential $e^{-2it\beta\omega_\gamma}$.

The state preparation costs for $|b_-\rangle$ and $|b_+\rangle$ depend on the register sizes and the regularity of the kernels.  Since the CoherentStep block-encodes a single $B_a$ (not the full $B = \sum_a B_a$), the per-jump quadrature analysis gives register sizes $r_- \approx 5$--$8$ and $r_+ \approx 10$--$15$ qubits for our parameter regime (see Quadrature errors for $B$, with the $|\mathcal{A}|$ factor replaced by $\|A_a\|^2 = 1/M_\mathcal{A}$).

At these moderate sizes, state preparation can be implemented without additional ancilla qubits via uniformly controlled rotations \cite{mottonen2004transformation,shende2006synthesis}: the preparation is decomposed into $r$ layers, each rotating one qubit conditioned on all previously prepared qubits, at a baseline cost of $\mathcal{O}(2^r)$ gates.  For smooth target functions --- including the Gaussian kernels $\bar f$, $b_-$, and $b_+^{(G)}$ --- the rotation angles at each layer vary smoothly with the conditioning value, so the uniformly controlled rotations can be compressed via Walsh--Hadamard truncation: retaining only the $\mathcal{O}(r^p)$ lowest-order Walsh coefficients (with $p \approx 2$--$3$ for near-quadratic angle functions) reduces the gate count per layer from $\mathcal{O}(2^k)$ to $\mathcal{O}(r^p)$, giving $\mathcal{O}(r^{p+1})$ total --- concretely $\mathcal{O}(r^3)$ for Gaussians, with zero ancillae.  For the Gaussian $b_+$, the phase $\arg(b_+) = -2\beta\omega_\gamma t$ is linear in $t$, implementable as a phase gradient with $\mathcal{O}(r)$ single-qubit $R_Z$ gates.  For the smooth Metropolis kernel $b_+^{(s,\eta)}$, the discontinuities at $t' = \pm\eta$ from the regularisation (5.43) prevent efficient Walsh truncation, so the full $\mathcal{O}(2^r)$ decomposition is needed --- still acceptable for $r \leq 15$.

Should the register sizes grow beyond $r \gtrsim 15$ --- for instance, at very small $\delta$ or in the fault-tolerant regime --- the QSP-based method of \cite{mcArdle2022quantum} achieves $\mathcal{O}(r \cdot \log(1/\varepsilon))$ gates at the cost of 3--4 ancilla qubits, by approximating the target amplitude function as a polynomial of the block-encoded position operator.  We defer further circuit-level details to Section 4 (Quantum Circuits).

\medskip

**The controlled evolutions.**  Between the two state preparations, controlled Hamiltonian evolutions realise the operator-valued integrands.  The overall structure is a sandwich: the outer evolution conjugates by $e^{\pm iH\bar t/\sigma}$, while the inner evolution implements the Heisenberg-picture expression $A^{a\dagger}(\beta\bar t')\,A^a(-\beta\bar t')$.  Let us trace through Algorithm 1a step by step.

*Outer conjugation* (steps 3 and 11).  Controlled on the outer register $T_-$ holding $|\bar t\rangle$, we apply the Trotterized evolution $S_2^{(M_-)}(-\bar t/\sigma)$ to the system in the forward pass (step 3), approximating $e^{-iH\bar t/\sigma}$.  In the reverse pass (step 11), the conjugate evolution $S_2^{(M_-)}(+\bar t/\sigma) \approx e^{+iH\bar t/\sigma}$ undoes this.  The Hamiltonian simulation time required for the outer integral is $\mathcal{O}(|t|/\sigma)$; since $b_-$ has effective width $\mathcal{O}(\beta\sigma)$, the maximum simulation time is $\mathcal{O}(\beta)$ (after setting $T_- = \Theta(\beta\sigma\log(1/\varepsilon))$ as dictated by the truncation bound (5.64)).

*Inner Heisenberg evolution* (steps 5--9).  The Heisenberg-picture product $A^{a\dagger}(\beta\bar t')\,A^a(-\beta\bar t')$ is not a single unitary, so it cannot be directly simulated.  Instead, we expand it using the definition $A(t) = e^{iHt}Ae^{-iHt}$:
$$
A^{a\dagger}(\beta\bar t')\,A^a(-\beta\bar t') = e^{i\beta H\bar t'}\,A_a^\dagger\,e^{-2i\beta H\bar t'}\,A_a\,e^{i\beta H\bar t'}.
$$
This triple product is implemented by three successive controlled Trotterized evolutions, with the jump operators $A_a^\dagger$ and $A_a$ applied in between:
\begin{enumerate}
\item Step 5: $S_2^{(M_+)}(+\beta\bar t')$ on $S$, controlled on $T_+$ \quad ($\approx e^{+i\beta H\bar t'}$),
\item Step 6: apply $A_a^\dagger$ on $S$,
\item Step 7: $S_2^{(M_+)}(-2\beta\bar t')$ on $S$, controlled on $T_+$ \quad ($\approx e^{-2i\beta H\bar t'}$),
\item Step 8: apply $A_a$ on $S$,
\item Step 9: $S_2^{(M_+)}(+\beta\bar t')$ on $S$, controlled on $T_+$ \quad ($\approx e^{+i\beta H\bar t'}$).
\end{enumerate}
The total Hamiltonian simulation time for the inner integral is $4\beta|\bar t'|$ per grid point.  Since the Gaussian envelope of $b_+$ (or $b_+^{(s,\eta)}$) confines the effective support to $|\bar t'| \lesssim T_+/2$, the maximum inner simulation time is $\mathcal{O}(\beta T_+)$.  For the Gaussian case, $T_+ = \Theta(\sqrt{\log(1/\varepsilon)}/(\beta\sigma))$, giving an inner Hamiltonian simulation time of $\mathcal{O}(\beta\sqrt{\log(1/\varepsilon)})$ per grid point.  For the smooth Metropolis case, $T_+ = \Theta(\sqrt{\log(1/\varepsilon)}/(\sigma\beta\sqrt{1+s}))$ from (5.76).

An important structural requirement, emphasised in Remark 11, is that the product formula $S_p^{(M)}$ used here must be *palindromic* (i.e.\ satisfy $S_p(t)^\dagger = S_p(-t)$).  The Strang splitting ($p = 2$) satisfies this automatically, as do all even-order symmetric product formulas.  The palindromic property is needed to preserve the OFT adjoint identity $\tilde{A}_{a'}(-\bar\omega)^\dagger = \tilde{A}_a(\bar\omega)$ at the Trotterized level, which in turn keeps the anti-Hermitian part of the quantum discriminant vanishing --- this is the structural identity that survives discretisation and Trotterisation, as shown in Remark 11.  A non-palindromic formula (e.g.\ first-order Lie--Trotter) would introduce an additional DB violation of the same order as the Trotter error, with a strictly larger constant prefactor.  For this reason, both the outer ($M_-$) and inner ($M_+$) controlled evolutions use the second-order Strang splitting throughout.

\medskip

**Uncomputation and the resulting block encoding** (steps 10--12).  After the inner evolution, the inner state preparation is undone: step 10 applies $\mathrm{StatePrep}^\dagger(T_+)$, mapping $|b_+\rangle \to |0\rangle$ and reflecting away the component orthogonal to $|0\rangle_{T_+}$.  Then step 11 undoes the outer Hamiltonian conjugation, and step 12 uncomputes the outer state preparation.  The net effect on the system register, projected onto the $|0\rangle_{T_-}|0\rangle_{T_+}$ ancilla subspace, is precisely the block-encoded action:
$$
\langle 0|_{T_-}\langle 0|_{T_+}\,U_{B_a}\,|0\rangle_{T_-}|0\rangle_{T_+} = \frac{B_a}{\alpha},
$$
with $\alpha = \alpha_-\,\alpha_+ = \|b_-\|_1\,\|b_+\|_1$.  The full circuit is symmetric (forward--inner--reverse), mirroring the sandwich structure of the LCU.

It is worth noting the analogy with the dissipative step: the DissipativeStep uses a *single* time register $\Omega$ to implement the Operator Fourier Transform (OFT, equation (5.48)), which is a single integral over $t$.  The CoherentStep uses *two* time registers $T_-, T_+$ for the nested double integral (5.61).  This nesting is the price of the Kossakowski factorisation (5.38), which splits the two-frequency dependence $\alpha_{\nu_1,\nu_2}$ into a product of one-frequency functions --- each of which requires its own time-domain register.

\medskip

**Subnormalisation $\alpha$ and its dependence on $\gamma$.**  The subnormalisation $\alpha = \|b_-\|_1\,\|b_+\|_1$ is a critical parameter: it enters both the QSP degree (see below) and the success probability of the block encoding.  Its value depends on the choice of transition weight:

- *Gaussian $\gamma_G$*:  From (5.40) and (5.41), $\|b_-\|_1 \leq (\pi/\sqrt{2})\,e^{\beta^2\sigma^2/8}$ and $\|b_+\|_1 = (\beta/\omega_\gamma)\sqrt{\sigma_\gamma/(2\pi)}$.  With the natural KMS parametrisation $\sigma = \omega_\gamma = \sigma_\gamma = 1/\beta$ (equation (5.20)), both norms are $\mathcal{O}(1)$, giving $\alpha = \mathcal{O}(1)$.

- *Smooth Metropolis $\gamma_M^{(s)}$*:  The outer norm $\|b_-\|_1$ is universal (independent of $\gamma$) and remains $\mathcal{O}(1)$ for $\sigma = 1/\beta$.  The inner norm $\|b_+^{(s,\eta)}\|_1$ acquires a logarithmic divergence from the $\eta$-regularisation (equation (5.46)):
$$
\|b_-\|_1\,\|b_+^{(s,\eta)}\|_1 = \mathcal{O}\!\left(\log\frac{\beta\|H\|\,\|\textstyle\sum_a A^{a\dagger}A^a\|}{\varepsilon}\right).
$$

This logarithmic overhead is the price paid for the broader Metropolis acceptance window and the $\eta$-regularisation of the $1/t$ singularity in $b_+$ (see the discussion following equation (5.43)).  Importantly, $\alpha$ enters the Hamiltonian simulation cost multiplicatively, so the overall coherent-term simulation time is $\mathcal{O}(\beta\log(1/\varepsilon))$ for the smooth Metropolis family, compared to $\mathcal{O}(\beta)$ for the Gaussian case.

\medskip

**Hamiltonian simulation via GQSP.**  Given the block encoding $U_{B_a}$ with $\langle 0|U_{B_a}|0\rangle = B_a/\alpha$, we wish to implement the unitary $e^{-i\delta B_a}$.  Since $U_{B_a}$ is a unitary that block-encodes a Hermitian operator $B_a/\alpha$ with $\|B_a/\alpha\| \leq 1$, its eigenvalues come in conjugate pairs $e^{\pm i\theta}$ where $\sin\theta = \lambda$ for each eigenvalue $\lambda$ of $B_a/\alpha$.  Thus, implementing $e^{-i\delta B_a}$ on the eigenspaces of $U_{B_a}$ reduces to finding a Laurent polynomial $P(e^{i\theta}) \approx e^{-i\delta\alpha\sin\theta}$ on the unit circle.

We use *Generalised Quantum Signal Processing* (GQSP) of Motlagh and Wiebe \cite{motlagh2024generalized} for this task; the basics of GQSP will be covered in Section 4 (Quantum Signal Processing).  Here we note only the features that are specific to our application.  Three properties of GQSP make it the natural choice over standard QSP \cite{low2017optimal,low2019hamiltonian}:
\begin{enumerate}
\item GQSP imposes no parity constraints on the polynomial $P$ --- important since $e^{-i\delta\alpha\sin\theta}$ has indefinite parity.
\item GQSP only requires controlled-$U_{B_a}$ (not $U_{B_a}^\dagger$), which simplifies the circuit.  (In the standard QSP framework, one would need the inverse block encoding, which would require running the entire LCU circuit in reverse with controlled phase conjugation.)
\item GQSP handles Laurent polynomials on the unit circle directly, which is the natural setting for unitary block encodings.
\end{enumerate}

The degree of the Laurent polynomial required for $\varepsilon_\mathrm{QSP}$-approximation of $e^{-i\delta\alpha\sin\theta}$ is given by the GQSP Hamiltonian simulation bound \cite[Section V]{motlagh2024generalized}:
$$
d = \mathcal{O}\!\left(\delta\,\alpha + \frac{\log(1/\varepsilon_\mathrm{QSP})}{\log\log(1/\varepsilon_\mathrm{QSP})}\right).
$$
The first term $\delta\alpha$ is the dominant contribution when the simulation time $\delta\alpha$ is large, representing the intrinsic cost of the Hamiltonian simulation (analogous to the degree of the Jacobi--Anger expansion $e^{-ix\sin\theta} = \sum_k J_k(x) e^{ik\theta}$, whose essential support has width $\sim x$).  The second term captures the cost of achieving high precision and is nearly linear in $\log(1/\varepsilon_\mathrm{QSP})$.  For our application, $\delta$ is the small Lindbladian step size and $\alpha = \mathcal{O}(1)$ for Gaussian weights (or $\mathcal{O}(\log(\beta\|H\|/\varepsilon))$ for smooth Metropolis), so the GQSP degree per step is moderate.

Each call to $U_{B_a}$ within the GQSP protocol requires one full forward-and-reverse pass through the block encoding (steps 2--12 of Algorithm 1a).  The total number of such calls per CoherentStep is $d$, so the total cost per CoherentStep is $d \times (M_- + 3M_+)$ Trotter steps plus $2d$ state preparations.

\medskip

**Trotter step budgets $M_-$ and $M_+$.**  The controlled Hamiltonian evolutions in the block encoding use two independent Trotter step counts: $M_-$ for the outer conjugation (steps 3 and 11, simulation time $\bar t/\sigma$) and $M_+$ for the inner Heisenberg evolution (steps 5, 7, 9, simulation time $\beta\bar t'$).  The Trotter errors from these two sources are quantified separately in Proposition 10, which bounds $\|\bar B - \tilde B\|$ in terms of the commutator-scaling constant $\tilde\alpha_\mathrm{comm}^{(2)}$ and the third absolute moments $\mu_3(|b_\pm|)$ of the weight functions.

This separation is a practical advantage of the coherent--dissipative splitting: since we implement $B$ and $\mathcal{L}_\mathrm{diss}$ as separate subroutines, we are free to choose $M_- \neq M_+$ (and indeed $M_\pm$ different from the Trotter step count $M$ used in the dissipative step).  The outer evolution involves the longer simulation times $\bar t/\sigma = \mathcal{O}(\beta)$ but acts on a smooth, rapidly decaying kernel $b_-$; the inner evolution involves the shorter simulation times $\beta\bar t'$ but acts on the potentially less regular kernel $b_+^{(s,\eta)}$.  Proposition 10 shows that, at leading order, both contributions scale as $\tilde\alpha_\mathrm{comm}^{(2)}/M^2$, weighted by their respective third moments --- so the Trotter step counts should be chosen to equalise the two error contributions.

\medskip

**Measure and reset** (step 14).  After the GQSP protocol has been applied, all ancilla registers --- $q_\mathrm{QSP}$, $T_+$, $T_-$ --- are measured and reset to $|0\rangle$.  Since the target function $e^{-i\delta\alpha\sin\theta}$ has modulus exactly one on the unit circle, the GQSP circuit is effectively deterministic: the constraint $|P(e^{i\theta})|^2 + |Q(e^{i\theta})|^2 = 1$ together with the fact that $P$ approximates a unit-modulus function forces $|Q| \approx 0$ everywhere, so the QSP signal qubit $q_\mathrm{QSP}$ is found in $|0\rangle$ with probability $1 - \mathcal{O}(\varepsilon_\mathrm{QSP})$ \cite[Corollary 8]{motlagh2024generalized}.  No amplitude amplification is needed.

\medskip

**Connection to the error budget.**  The CoherentStep contributes to the overall error budget through several channels.  Per Lindbladian step, the coherent--dissipative splitting (5.90) introduces an $\mathcal{O}(\delta^2)$ error from the first-order product formula $e^{\delta\mathcal{L}_{a,\mathrm{coh}}}e^{\delta\mathcal{L}_{a,\mathrm{diss}}} = e^{\delta\mathcal{L}_a + \mathcal{O}(\delta^2)}$.  Within the CoherentStep itself, three additional error sources arise: the quadrature (discretisation) error $\|B - \bar B\|$, controlled by the qubit counts $r_-$ and $r_+$ as analysed in the Quadrature errors for $B$ subsection (equations (5.64)--(5.68) for the outer integral, (5.75) and (5.78) for the inner integral); the Trotterisation error $\|\bar B - \tilde B\|$, controlled by $M_-$ and $M_+$ via Proposition 10; and the GQSP approximation error $\varepsilon_\mathrm{QSP}$, controlled by the polynomial degree $d$.  All of these are set to be $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon)$ individually, and the total error is bounded by the triangle inequality.  We consolidate the full error budget in the Error budget and total complexity discussion that follows.

*[Insert Algorithm 1a here.]*

## Writing Notes
<!-- These notes are for the author, not for the thesis -->

- **Equation cross-references to verify in TeX**: (5.28), (5.29), (5.30), (5.38), (5.39), (5.40), (5.41), (5.43), (5.46), (5.61), (5.64), (5.68), (5.75), (5.76), (5.78), (5.81), (5.90), (5.20), (5.48). Also Proposition 10, Remark 11, and the Time, time, time / Quadrature errors for $B$ / Generator splitting subsection references.
- **Citations to verify**: `\cite{chen2023efficient}` = CKG23, `\cite{chen2023quantum}` = CKBG23, `\cite{motlagh2024generalized}` = MW24, `\cite{childs2012hamiltonian}` = Childs \& Wiebe 2012 "Ham.\ sim.\ using LCU" and `\cite{berry2015simulating}` = Berry et al.\ 2015 "Truncated Taylor series" for original LCU technique, `\cite{gilyen2019quantum}` = Gily\'en, Su, Low, Wiebe 2019 "QSVT for Ham.\ sim.\ and beyond" for block-encoding formalism (Def.\ 43, Lemma 45), `\cite{low2017optimal}` and `\cite{low2019hamiltonian}` for standard QSP, `\cite{mcArdle2022quantum}` for QSP-based state preparation (fault-tolerant regime), `\cite{mottonen2004transformation}` = Möttönen et al. 2004 and `\cite{shende2006synthesis}` = Shende--Bullock--Markov 2006 for uniformly controlled rotations, `\cite{grover2002creating}` = Grover--Rudolph 2002 for log-concave state prep.
- **Notation check**: The thesis uses $b_1, b_2$ in the CKG paper but $b_-, b_+$ in the thesis itself. Confirm consistent use of thesis notation throughout.
- **Review feedback addressed**:
  - (1.4) Complex sgn for $b_+$: explicitly stated that sgn means unit-modulus phase for complex kernels.
  - (2.2) Product formula order: consistently use $S_2^{(M_\pm)}$ (Strang splitting) throughout, with explicit justification via Remark 11.
  - (3.4) QSP explanation: kept brief since Ch 4 will cover basics; focused on the three reasons GQSP is chosen and the specific degree bound.
  - (3.8) Role of coherent step (CKG vs CKBG): opening paragraph explicitly states this.
  - (4.3) LCU citation: included \cite{childs2012hamiltonian,berry2015simulating}.
  - (4.5) Cross-references to earlier subsections: extensive references to Time, time, time; Quadrature errors for $B$; Generator splitting; Remark 11; Propositions 9 and 10.
- **Figures needed**: A circuit diagram analogous to CKG23 Figure 4 but showing the full nested structure with both $T_-$ and $T_+$ registers would be very valuable. This would be the thesis's own version of Figure 4.
- **[RESOLVED]**: State preparation strategy clarified.  Per-jump register sizes are $r_- \approx 5$--$8$, $r_+ \approx 10$--$15$ (the $|\mathcal{A}|$ factor drops from the thesis formulas since we block-encode a single $B_a$).  At these sizes, zero-ancilla uniformly controlled rotations ($\mathcal{O}(2^r)$ gates) suffice and are subdominant to Hamiltonian simulation.  For the Gaussian $b_+$: Grover--Rudolph + phase gradient gives $\mathcal{O}(r^2)$ gates, zero ancillae.  For the Metropolis $b_+^{(s,\eta)}$: the $\eta$-regularisation discontinuities prevent Walsh--Hadamard truncation savings, so the full $\mathcal{O}(2^r)$ decomposition is needed --- still fine for $r \leq 15$.  McArdle et al. \cite{mcArdle2022quantum} ($\mathcal{O}(r \cdot \log(1/\varepsilon))$ gates + 3--4 ancillae) becomes preferable only for $r \gtrsim 15$.  Added citations: \cite{mottonen2004transformation}, \cite{shende2006synthesis}, \cite{grover2002creating}.
- **[RESOLVED]**: The GQSP success probability is $1 - \mathcal{O}(\varepsilon_\mathrm{QSP})$ because the target function $e^{-i\delta\alpha\sin\theta}$ has unit modulus. Confirmed via MW24 Corollary 8 and Low \& Chuang 2017 Eq. 11. No amplitude amplification needed.
- **Connection points**: The error budget consolidation (next paragraph in the thesis) should pull together all the $r_-, r_+, M_-, M_+, d$ parameters. The DissipativeStep prose (to follow) mirrors this structure with a single time register $\Omega$.
